name: Salvadoran Civil War
summary: The Salvadoran Civil War was fought between the U.S.-backed Salvadoran government and security forces, allied paramilitaries and death squads, and the FMLN guerrilla coalition. The UN-backed Truth Commission documented systematic state and insurgent abuses, with the overwhelming majority of registered serious-violence complaints attributed to state agents and allied forces. Modern statistical work estimates about 71,629 civilian killings and disappearances during 1980–1992, with substantial uncertainty.

sides.name: Government of El Salvador and allied forces

military_killed.notes: Reported as 10,360+ government-side combatants killed; treat as a minimum/point estimate, not a firm upper bound. The Truth Commission report notes annex lists of armed-forces members killed, but the accessible report text did not provide a total.

breakdown.cause: extrajudicial execution, enforced disappearance, massacre, death-squad assassination, bombing and shelling
breakdown.victims_group: Salvadoran civilians, especially suspected FMLN sympathizers, peasants in conflict zones, political opponents, clergy, labor activists, students, teachers, journalists and human-rights workers
breakdown.notes: Inferred by applying the Truth Commission's 'almost 85%' state/allied attribution share to Hoover Green & Ball's estimated 60,326–83,775 civilian killings and disappearances. This is not a direct demographic side-attribution estimate.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: The Truth Commission attributed almost 85% of registered cases to state agents, allied paramilitary groups, and death squads. Because the Commission's data are complaints and not a full mortality database, the numeric range is a proportional inference.

deaths_from_actions.notes: Government counterinsurgency tactics produced mass displacement and refugee flight, but no reliable separately quantified indirect-death estimate was found. Violent deaths from massacres, executions, shelling and bombing are counted under civilians_killed_directly.

military_killed.notes: Seligson and McElhinny are reported as estimating 12,274–23,840 FMLN combatant deaths; another common summary gives 12,274–20,000.

breakdown.cause: extrajudicial execution, assassination, disappearance, kidnapping, bombings, land mines
breakdown.victims_group: mayors, public officials, suspected informers, alleged traitors, right-wing supporters, judges, civilians in FMLN-controlled areas, and civilians harmed by mines
breakdown.notes: Inferred by applying the Truth Commission's approximately 5% FMLN attribution share to Hoover Green & Ball's 60,326–83,775 civilian killings/disappearances estimate. The Truth Commission registered more than 800 serious-violence complaints attributed to FMLN and about 300 disappearance complaints in areas of FMLN control, but did not verify a general FMLN leadership directive for disappearances.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: The FMLN was responsible for a much smaller but real share of documented abuses. The range is a proportional inference from complaint attribution and the modern civilian-death estimate.

totals.notes: The authoritative UN Truth Commission report established the accepted death toll at more than 75,000. The military low sums 10,360 government-side deaths and 12,274 FMLN deaths. The civilian total is inferred by subtracting the military minimum from the 75,000 total.

key_atrocities.name: El Mozote massacre and surrounding villages
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Government of El Salvador and allied forces
key_atrocities.victims_group: civilians in El Mozote and nearby villages
key_atrocities.notes: The Truth Commission reported more than 500 identified victims at El Mozote and surrounding villages, with many others unidentified; later reporting and legal/forensic work commonly cites about 900–1,200 victims or at least 986 identified victims.

key_atrocities.name: Sumpul River massacre
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Government of El Salvador and allied forces
key_atrocities.notes: The Truth Commission found no less than 300 unarmed civilians massacred; survivors' judicial complaint referred to the murder of 600 people.

key_atrocities.name: El Calabozo massacre
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Government of El Salvador and allied forces
key_atrocities.victims_group: men, women and children held prisoner
key_atrocities.notes: The Truth Commission found that Atlacatl Battalion troops killed over 200 men, women and children whom they were holding prisoner.

key_atrocities.name: UCA Jesuit murders
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Government of El Salvador and allied forces
key_atrocities.victims_group: six Jesuit priests, a cook, and her 16-year-old daughter
key_atrocities.notes: The Truth Commission found that senior military officers ordered the killing of Father Ignacio Ellacuría and that no witnesses be left; eight people were killed.

key_atrocities.name: FMLN executions of mayors
key_atrocities.notes: The Truth Commission described 11 executions in detail and stated there were more; it rejected FMLN arguments that the mayors were legitimate military targets.

uncertainty_notes: The major uncertainties are definitional and methodological. The Truth Commission provides attribution percentages for complaints, not a complete death census; Hoover Green & Ball provide the best modern estimate of civilian killings and disappearances, but not a clean perpetrator-by-perpetrator allocation; Seligson & McElhinny provide older combatant and total-death estimates summarized in secondary sources. Indirect deaths from displacement, hunger, disease, or exposure are not separately quantified in the sources found, so they are not included as numeric estimates.

sources.title: From Madness to Hope: The 12-Year War in El Salvador
sources.publisher: United Nations Commission on the Truth for El Salvador / USIP
